---
title: "Temporal Trends in RSV Wastewater Concentrations Across Vermont NWSS Monitoring
  Sites, 2023-26"
author: Jacob Pancoast | University of Vermont<br>B.S. Candidate, Computer Science & Epidemiology Minor
date: "June 10, 2026"
output:
  html_document: default
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}

pacman::p_load(gratia,
               tidyverse, 
               tsibble,
               slider,
               fable,
               feasts,
               Kendall,
               mgcv,
               knitr,
               kableExtra,
               patchwork,
               cowplot)

opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE)

```

``` {r define_constants, include=FALSE}

body = 16

font_sizes <- 
  c(
    body = 16,
    h1 = 2 * body,
    h2 = 1.7 * body,
    h3 = 1.4 * body,
    plot_title = 0.8 * body,
    plot_facet = 0.6 * body,
    plot_text = 0.4 * body
  )

```

``` {css, include=FALSE}

h1, h2, h3, h4 {
  font-family: Georgia, serif;
}

h1 {
  font-size: 2em
}

h2 {
  font-size: 1.6em
}

h3 {
  font-size: 1.3em
}

body {
  font-family: 'Open Sans', sans-serif;
  font-size: 16px;
  line-height: 1.5;
}

``` 

<div style ="background-color: #d6d6d6; padding: 10px;">
# Summary

### What is already known about this topic?

In the United States, RSV seasonality has been disrupted following the COVID-19 pandemic. The 2025-26 RSV season was delayed but carried no more significant burden of disease. Temperate regions are subject to harsher, more condensed RSV seasons.

### What is added by this report?

Vermont has low population density and high risk for harsh RSV seasons. Vermont contributes minimally to national averages, and may be overlooked in national assessments of RSV burden. The 2025-26 season
</div>

# Introduction

## Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV)

Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is an RNA virus belonging to the *Pneumoviridae* family of viruses. RSV infection is most often accompanied by difficulty breathing, coughing, sneezing, congestion, and decreased appetite. RSV can lead to more severe illnesses including [bronchiolitis](https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/bronchiolitis/symptoms-causes/syc-20351565) and [pneumonia](https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/pneumonia/symptoms-causes/syc-20354204), more commonly in high-risk groups such as infants, young children, and older adults^1^. In the United States, RSV activity peaks during respiratory virus season. Typically, cases increase in the fall, peak around December or January, and decrease into the spring^1,2^.

Clinically, RSV presents similarly to the common cold, influenza, and COVID-19. As a result, RSV infection is frequently misdiagnosed, especially in high risk populations^3^. Worldwide, RSV is a leading cause of acute lower respiratory infections among both children and elderly. In the United States alone, RSV was estimated to cause between 60,000 and 160,000 hospitalizations as well as 6,000-10,000 deaths annually among adults aged 65 and over in 2023^4^.

``` {r flu_burden, fig.topcaption = TRUE, fig.cap = "**Figure 1.** Estimated ranges of annual flu and RSV burdens. Figure 1A was obtained from the CDC and shows flu burden from 2010-25^5^. Figure 1B was created using data from a CDC MMWR report on RSV from 2023 estimating the annual burden."}

flu_rsv_burdens <- 
  plot_spacer() +
  
  (
    ggdraw() +
      draw_image("figures/flu-burden.png") +
      draw_label(label = "Flu Burden", x = 0.5, y = 0.9) +
      draw_label(label = "Figure 1A. Estimated ranges of annual\nflu burden from 2010-25.\nSource: CDC Estimated Flu Burden⁵", x = 0.5, y = 0.1, size = 10)
    ) +
  
  plot_spacer() +
  
  (
    ggdraw() +
     draw_image("figures/rsv-burden.png") +
      draw_label(label = "RSV Burden", x = 0.5, y = 0.9) +
      draw_label("Figure 1B. Created using estimated ranges of annual\n RSV burden in 2023.\nSource: CDC MMWR on Use of RSV Vaccines; July, 2023⁴", x= 0.5, y = 0.1, size = 10)
    ) +
  
  plot_spacer() +
  
  plot_layout(
    widths = c(0.2, 1, 0.3, 1, 0.2)
  )

flu_rsv_burdens

```

The general hospitalization rate for RSV may be between 2 - 4.4 times lower than influenza, while the mortality rate may be between 1.05 - 5.2 times lower than influenza (Figure 1). Among elderly populations, RSV has been shown to exhibit higher rates of pneumonia, hospitalization, and extended hospital stays compared to the flu^6^. Lowering the incidence of RSV for these at-risk groups is critical to improve health outcomes and reduce costs associated with prolonged hospital stays.

## RSV Monitoring Systems

The RSV-Hospitalization Surveillance Network (RSV-NET) is a CDC-led program that monitors laboratory-confirmed RSV hospitalizations to estimate disease burden and identify trends in virus circulation^7^. Established in 2018, this program serves 14 states (found [here](https://www.cdc.gov/resp-net/dashboard/)) and over 170 counties. The National Respiratory and Eneteric Virus Surveillance System (NREVSS) measures weekly positive RSV tests since 1989 through voluntarily participating labs. In low-population states, including Vermont, RSV monitoring suffers from undercoverage and the lack of a nationwide surveillance system. Most states are not involved in RSV-NET and have few NREVSS labs.

However, gaps in clinical surveillance may be supplemented by wastewater surveillance. The CDC collects wastewater data reported by state and local health departments through the National Wastewater Surveillance System (NWSS)^8^.

Wastewater based epidemiology (WBE) is an important tool in assessing respiratory viral disease burden ^2,9-11^ that has gained traction as an epidemiological method since the COVID-19 pandemic^12^ despite being in practice for over 80 years^13^. While clinical surveillance remains valuable in populations with high access to testing^14^, WBE offers advantages in that it is not subject to the same delays and constraints as clinical testing ^2,9,11^; it is also less invasive, cost effective^11^, and may be generalizable to neighboring communities^2^. Without the reliance on testing access, WBE may better indicate viral disease burden ^2,9,10^ in lower-income communities while also accounting for asymptomatic individuals, which may represent around 17% of cases^15^.

As of 2022, RSV testing was generally not recommended for children in Vermont since it does not significantly alter treatment outcomes^16^. Because publicly available RSV testing and hospitalization data are scarce for Vermont, WBE is a critical tool in understanding RSV epidemiology in the state. 

``` {r NWSS_sites, fig.topcaption = TRUE, fig.cap = "**Figure 2**. United States NWSS Sites reporting RSV concentrations as of May 14, 2026. Source: CDC Wastewater Monitoring Program^17^.", out.width = "600px"}

include_graphics("figures/nwss_sites.png")

```

Sites across the country (Figure 2) are selected by local governments, based on site effectiveness and population coverage. Many health departments prioritize enrolling sites with a high social vulnerability index. 89% of participating NWSS sites are located at a wastewater treatment plant^17^. In Vermont, the number of NWSS-participating wastewater treatment sites is low (n = 6) compared to states with similar population density (Minnesota, n = 29; West Virginia, n = 17)^18^. Undercoverage in Vermont may affect surveillance and health outcomes. 

Wastewater collection differs between sites. Some sites may sample untreated wastewater (sewage and stormwater) or primary sludge (suspended solids from wastewater). Sampling methods also differ, including grab samples (from a point in time) or composite samples (aggregated grab samples). Sampling frequency also differs between sites^17^. For each sample, various metrics are recorded including flow rate, virus type, viral quantification, and contamination measurements. Each site is also associated with a region and population served^8,17^. Viral quantification is measured using repeated PCR tests, and all data is submitted to the CDC.

## RSV in Vermont

``` {r NREVSS_dash, fig.topcaption = TRUE, fig.cap = "**Figure 3**. Percentage of positive RSV tests by state as of the week ending April 25, 2026. Source: CDC NREVSS Dashboard^18^.", out.width = "800px"}

include_graphics("figures/rsv_quintiles.png")

```

In the 2025-26 season, the CDC identified RSV activity started later in most parts of the US, and also peaked later^19^. As of late April 2026, Vermont had one of the highest proportions of positive RSV tests in the country (Figure 3). This report investigates temporal trends across Vermont's six NWSS monitoring sites from 2023-26 to assess the burden of the 2025-26 RSV season to evaluate how well the CDC's assessment of the 2025-26 season^20^ generalizes to Vermont and discuss Vermont-specific response to RSV.

# Methods

CDC wastewater data for RSV was obtained from Data.CDC.gov. Each row represents a wastewater sample for RSV from participating NWSS sites, including dates of collection^21^. The "pcr_target_avg_conc" column was chosen for analysis of RSV concentrations rather than "pcr_target_flowpop_lin" since the flow-population-normalized column had large amounts of missing data. Population differences between sites were small enough to accommodate this decision (Table S1). Before analysis, the data was cleaned and visualized. During this step, a seven-day rolling average concentration was calculated for each data point to reduce noise and artifacts. This average was used for all further analysis. 

All data cleaning, analyses, and visualizations were conducted using R (version 4.5.2; R Foundation).

``` {r clean_data, include=FALSE}
vt_rsv <- 
  read.csv("CDC_RSV_Wastewater_Data.csv") |> 
  
  select(site, counties_served, sample_collect_date, pcr_target, pcr_target_avg_conc, lod_sewage, population_served) |>
  
  filter(pcr_target == 'rsv',
         site != '1962') |> 
  
  # Since we filtered for pcr_target == RSV, we can drop the pcr_target column
  select(-pcr_target) |> 
  
  rename(county = counties_served,
         date = sample_collect_date,
         rsv_conc = pcr_target_avg_conc) |> 
  
  mutate(date = ymd(date), # date
         rsv_conc = as.numeric(str_replace_all(rsv_conc, ",", "")), # convert to a numeric column and remove commas
         county = as.factor(county), # convert to a factor
         site = as.factor(site), # convert to a factor
         facet_label = paste0("Site ", site, ": ", county, " county"),
         lod_sewage = as.numeric(str_replace_all(lod_sewage, ",", ""))
         ) |>  # create a title column
  
  arrange(.by = date)

```

``` {r rolling_avg, include=FALSE}

# Calculate the rolling average column for data visualization

vt_rsv <-
  vt_rsv |> 
  
  group_by(site) |> # group by site for calculating rolling average
  
  arrange(date, .by_group = TRUE) |> # arrange by date for calculating rolling average
  
  mutate(mean_7day = slide_index_dbl(
           .x = rsv_conc,
           .i = date,
           .f = ~mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
           .before = days(6)
         )) |> # create the rolling average column
  
  ungroup() # ungroup the data

```

```{r exploratory_analysis}

exploratory <- 
  ggplot(
    data = vt_rsv
  ) +
  geom_line(
    mapping = aes(
      x = date,
      y = rsv_conc
      )#,
    #width = 1
  ) +
  geom_line(
    mapping = aes(
      x = date,
      y = mean_7day,
      color = site
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  theme() +
  theme_minimal() +
  facet_wrap(
    facets = vars(facet_label),
    ncol = 2,
    scales = 'free_y'
  ) + labs(
    title = NULL,
    y = "RSV Concentration",
    x = "Date",
    color = "Site number",
    caption = "Source: CDC National Wastewater Surveillance System¹¹"
  ) + theme(
    strip.text = element_text(size = font_sizes["plot_facet"])
  )

```

``` {r graph_colors}

data <- 
  ggplot_build(exploratory)$data[[2]]

colors <- 
  unique(data$colour)

# Define colors
names(colors) <- 
  c(
    1965,
    1966,
    1967,
    1975,
    1976,
    2350
  )

```

### Site Comparison

Each sample was classified by season (starting in the second half of one year, through the first half of the following year). Respiratory virus season usually lasts October through May, but a broader range was used to observe any changes in seasonality for the 2025-26 season. Without benchmark clinical indicators to define seasonality ^14,22^, season onset and offset were defined using the methodology for WBE outlined in Boehm et. al ^2,23,24^. For each individual site, season onset was defined as the first day in the second half of the year where 100% of samples collected in a 14 day look back period had an RSV concentration equal to or above 2 times the limit of detection for that site. Season offset was defined as the first day following a season onset date where 50% or less of the samples collected in a 14 day lookback had concentrations above or equal to 2 times the limit of detection for that site. This method was originally described for sites that measure viral concentration in copies per dry gram of sludge. For this analysis, this definition was extended to sites measuring copies per liter of wastewater. This was a feasible extrapolation since the threshold proportion was preserved by using the individual limit of detection for each site.

From this data, tables were constructed to compare season lengths, peak days, peak years for each site, and season onsets and offsets.

### Peak Comparison

Peaks analysis was performed to identify per-site and per-season peaks across the data. 

Additional temporal information was added to each row, including "days elapsed since season onset", and "days elapsed since last anchor date". To compare offsets in seasonality, June 1st was selected as the anchor date since it never fell in-season. From this data, graphs comparing seasonal peaks were constructed. 

``` {r seasonal_cols, include=FALSE}

vt_rsv_seasonal <- 
  vt_rsv |> 
  arrange(.by = date) |> 
  
  group_by(site) |> 
  
  # Add season columns
  mutate(
    
    # Is this date in season onset range?
    onset_criteria = slide_index2_lgl(
      .x = rsv_conc,
      .y = lod_sewage,
      .i = date,
      # Onset criteria is defined as dates where all data within last
      # 14 days is above 2 * LOD
      .f = ~ all(.x > (2 * .y)),
      .before = days(14)
      ),
    
    offset_criteria = slide_index2_lgl(
      .x = rsv_conc,
      .y = lod_sewage,
      .i = date,
      # Offset criteria is defined as dates where only ~50% of samples during
      # last 14 days had concentrations over 2 * LOD
      .f = ~ mean(.x > (2 * .y), na.rm = TRUE) < 0.5,
      .before = days(14)
    ),
    
    # Add the season column
    season = case_when(
      # If in 2nd half of the year XX, the season is XX - X(X+1)
      month(date) %in% 7:12 ~ paste0(year(date), "_", year(date)+1),
      # If in 1st half of the year XX, the season is X(X-1) - XX
      month(date) %in% 1:6 ~ paste0(year(date)-1, "_", year(date)),
      ),
    
    # Starting year of the season
    starting_year = case_when(
      month(date) %in% 7:12 ~ year(date),
      month(date) %in% 1:6 ~ year(date) - 1,
    )
  ) |> 
  
  ungroup() |> 
  
  group_by(site, season) |> 
  
  # Get onset date and conclusion date of each season
  mutate(
    
    # Onset date is defined as the first day in the second half of the year where all samples
    # collected in a 14 day prior period have concentrations above 2 * LOD
    onset = if (any(onset_criteria)) {
      
      # If there is any onset date, we have a minimum to pick as the onset
      min(date[onset_criteria])
      
    } else {
      
      as.Date(NA)
      
    },
    
    # Offset date is defined as the first day after onset where 50% of the concentrations
    # in the previous 14 days were below 2 * LOD
    offset = if (any(offset_criteria & !is.na(onset) & date > onset)) {
      
      # If there is an offset date that exists
      min(date[offset_criteria & date > onset])
      
    } else {
      
      as.Date(NA)
      
    }
    
  ) |> 
  
  mutate(
    
    # Adjust periods outside of season offsets and offsets to off-season
    season = case_when(
      
      # Missing onset
      is.na(onset) ~ "Off-Season",
      
      # Ongoing season
      is.na(offset) & date >= as.Date(onset) ~ season,
      is.na(offset) & date < as.Date(onset) ~ "Off-Season",
      
      # Normal season
      date >= as.Date(onset) & date <= as.Date(offset) ~ season,
      
      # Outside a normal season
      date < as.Date(onset) | date > as.Date(offset) ~ "Off-Season",
      
      # Error catching
      TRUE ~ 'Error'
    )
    
  ) |> 

  mutate(
    
    # Day of the current season
    day_of_season = if_else(
      season != 'Off-Season',
      as.numeric(date - onset),
      as.numeric(NA)
      ),
    
    # DAys since off season
    days_since_june_first = if_else(
      season != 'Off-Season',
      as.numeric(date - make_date(year = starting_year, month = 6, day = 1)),
      as.numeric(NA)
    )
    
  ) |> 
  
  ungroup()

```

``` {r first_collection_date}

first_collection_date <- 
  min(vt_rsv_seasonal$date)

```

``` {r plot_season_trend}

# Seasonal data by days since start of season
season_trend <- 
  ggplot(
  data = vt_rsv_seasonal |> filter(site %in% c(1965, 1966, 1975),
                                   season != "Off-Season")
) +
  geom_line(
    mapping = aes(
      x = day_of_season,
      y = mean_7day,
      color = season
    )
  ) +
  scale_color_discrete(labels = c("2022-23", "2023-24", "2024-25", "2025-26")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  theme_minimal() +
  facet_wrap(
    facets = vars(facet_label),
    ncol = 2,
    scales = 'free_y'
  ) +
  labs(
    title = NULL,
    y = "Seven-Day Rolling Average of RSV Concentration",
    x = "Days Since Start of Season",
    color = "Season",
    caption = "Source: CDC National Wastewater Surveillance System¹¹"
  ) + theme(
    strip.text = element_text(size = font_sizes["facet_title"])
  )

```

``` {r plot_season_offset}

# Graph offset between seasons
season_offset <- 
  ggplot(
    data = vt_rsv_seasonal |> filter(site %in% c(1965, 1966, 1975),
                                     season != "Off-Season")
  ) +
  geom_line(
    mapping = aes(
      x = days_since_june_first,
      y = mean_7day,
      color = season
    )
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_color_discrete(labels = c("2022-23", "2023-24", "2024-25", "2025-26")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  facet_wrap(
    facets = vars(facet_label),
    ncol = 2,
    scales = 'free_y'
  ) +
  labs(
    title = NULL,
    y = "Seven-Day Rolling Average of RSV Concentrations",
    x = "Days Since June 1st of Previous Off-Season",
    color = "Season",
    caption = "Source: CDC National Wastewater Surveillance System¹¹"
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = font_sizes["plot_title"],
                              hjust = 0.5),
    strip.text = element_text(size = font_sizes["facet_title"])
  )

```

``` {r create_tables, include=FALSE}

# Compare peak days across sites and seasons
peak_days <- 
  vt_rsv_seasonal |> 
  
  group_by(season, site) |> 

  summarise(
    peak_day = onset[which.max(mean_7day)] + days(day_of_season[which.max(mean_7day)]),
  ) |> 
  
  filter(season != 'Off-Season') |> 
  
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = season,
    values_from = peak_day
  )

peak_days <- 
  peak_days |> 
  
  bind_rows(
    
    peak_days |>
      
      summarise(site = "avg_peak_day_per_season",
                across(-site, ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))
  ) |> 
  
  rowwise() |> 
  
  mutate(
    avg = format(
      as.Date(
        mean(
          as.numeric(format(c_across(-site), "%j")), 
          na.rm = TRUE
          )
        ), 
      "%m-%d"
      )
    )

  # Compare peak year for each site
peak_year <- 
  vt_rsv_seasonal |> 
  
  filter(season != 'Off-Season') |> 
  
  summarise(
    year = season[which.max(mean_7day)],
    .by = c(site)
  )

# Mean season lengths across sites
szn_lengths <- 
  vt_rsv_seasonal |> 
  
  distinct(site, season, onset, offset) |> 
  
  filter(season != 'Off-Season') |>
  
  mutate(
    
    # Season lengths
    season_length = if_else(
      is.na(offset),
      max(vt_rsv$date) - onset,
      offset - onset
    )
    
  ) |> 
  
  select(-onset, -offset) |> 
  
  pivot_wider(names_from = season,
              values_from = season_length) |> 
  
  rowwise() |>

  mutate(
      
    avg_szn_length_per_site = mean(c_across(-site), na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_szn_length_per_site_excl_22_23 = mean(c_across(-c(site, '2022_2023')), na.rm = TRUE)
    
  ) |> 
  
  ungroup()

szn_lengths <- 
  szn_lengths |>
  
  bind_rows(
    
    # Get season length per year
    szn_lengths |> 
      
    summarise(
    
      site = "avg_szn_length_per_year",
      across(-site, ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE))
             
    ),
    
    #Get season length per year excluding low data
    szn_lengths |>
      
      filter(site %in% c('1965', '1966', '1975')) |> 

      summarise(

        site = "avg_szn_length_per_year_excl_low_data",
        across(-site, ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE))

      )
    
  ) |> 
  
  mutate(
    
    across(-site, ~ round(as.numeric(.), digits = 2))
    
  )

onset_offset <- 
  
  vt_rsv_seasonal |> 
  
  distinct(site, season, onset, offset) |> 
  
  filter(season != "Off-Season") |> 
  
  group_by(season) |> 
  
  summarise(average_onset = mean(onset, na.rm = TRUE),
            average_offset = mean(offset, na.rm = TRUE))

```

### Seasonal Decomposition

``` {r seasonal_decomposition_func, include=FALSE}

seasonal_decomposition <- function(the_site) {
  
  return(
    vt_rsv |> 
    
    filter(site == the_site) |>
    
    select(date, rsv_conc) |>
    
    mutate(week = floor_date(date, 'week'), # Change dates to per week
           rsv_conc = log1p(rsv_conc)) |> # Transform rsv_conc by log
    
    group_by(week) |> 
    summarise(weekly_mean = mean(rsv_conc, na.rm = TRUE)) |> # Create weekly mean
    
    as_tsibble(index=week) |> # Convert to tsibble
    
    fill_gaps() |> 
    
    mutate(
      weekly_mean = replace_na(weekly_mean, 0)
    )
    )
}

```

For all further analysis, only the three sites containing consistent data spanning *$n \ge 3$* years were used (sites 1965, 1966, and 1975) due to inadequate coverage in the remaining sites.

Seasonal-Trend decomposition using Loess (STL) decomposes a time series ($y_t$) into trend-cycle ($T_t$), seasonal ($S_t$), and remainder ($R_t$) components such that $y_t$ = $T_t$ + $S_t$ + $R_t$. Classical decomposition may also be multiplicative, however STL only supports additive decomposition. Using this method, graphs of each component per site were created to compare trends across sites.

For this data, a multiplicative decomposition was more appropriate due to proportional variation in the magnitude of seasonal fluctuations over the time series. Since STL only supports an additive decomposition, concentrations were log transformed to convert the multiplicative structure to additive, while also normalizing the right skew in the data.

Concentrations were then grouped by week and averaged to create the regular weekly intervals required for decomposition. Data gaps in site 1966 were filled with *concentration = 0* for the weeks of April 21, 2024; April 28, 2024; and May 5; 2024. 

``` {r seasonal_decomposition, include=FALSE}

# Get seasonal decomposition for the three compatible sites
site_1965 <- seasonal_decomposition('1965') |> 
  model(stl = STL(weekly_mean)) |>  # Fit model
  ungroup()

site_1966 <- seasonal_decomposition('1966') |> 
  model(stl = STL(weekly_mean)) |>  # Fit model
  ungroup()

site_1975 <- seasonal_decomposition('1975') |> 
  model(stl = STL(weekly_mean)) |>  # Fit model
  ungroup()

```

``` {r get_decomposition_peaks, include=FALSE}

# Extract_decomposition_peaks returns the peak weeks for a site
# from seasonal decomposition

extract_decomposition_peaks <- 
  function(site) {
    
    decomp_peaks <- 
      components(site) |> 
      
      index_by(year = ~ year(.)) |> 
      
      summarise(
        week_max = week[which.max(season_year)], # Get the peak week per season
        max_season_year = max(season_year, na.rm = TRUE) # Get the value of the max peak
        )
    
    return(decomp_peaks)
  }

```

``` {r get_peaks_from_decomposition, include=FALSE}

decomp_peaks_1965 <- 
  extract_decomposition_peaks(site_1965)
  
decomp_peaks_1966 <- 
  extract_decomposition_peaks(site_1966)

decomp_peaks_1975 <- 
  extract_decomposition_peaks(site_1975)

```

``` {r graph_seasonal_decomp, include=FALSE}

# Graph seasonal decomposition for site 1965
decomposed_1965 <- 
  components(site_1965) |> 
  autoplot(
    color = colors["1965"]
  ) +
  labs(
    title = NULL,
    x = "Week",
    caption = "Source: CDC National Wastewater Surveillance System"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Graph seasonal decomposition for site 1966
decomposed_1966 <- 
  components(site_1966) |> 
  autoplot(
    color = colors["1966"]
  ) +
  labs(
    subtitle = "Site 1966: Chittenden County, VT",
    x = "Week"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() 

# Graph seasonal decomposition for site 1975
decomposed_1975 <- 
  components(site_1975) |> 
  autoplot(
    color = colors["1975"]
  ) +
  labs(
    subtitle = "Site 1975: Washington County, VT",
    x = "Week"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

```

``` {r ymd_to_md, include=FALSE}

# Peak_as_month_date takes a decomp_peaks_(site number) and returns the mean peak date
# as a month-date format

peak_as_month_date <- 
  function(peaks) {
  
    max_weeks <- 
      (peaks |> 
         
         filter(
           year != 2023 # Filter out 2023 with truncated data
           ) |> 
         
         mutate(
           week_max = make_date(
             year = case_when(
               month(week_max) < 6 ~ 2024, # Standardize year for the sake of averaging
               month(week_max) > 6 ~ 2023
             ), 
             month = month(week_max), # Keep the month and date
             day = day(week_max)
             )
           )
       )$week_max # Extract only the week_max
    
    mean = mean(max_weeks) # Calculate the mean peak date
    
    return(format(mean, "%m-%d"))
  }
```

### Generalized Additive Model (GAM)

STL decomposition identified that long-term trend and seasonality were the dominant sources of variation in Vermont's RSV wastewater concentration, informing predictor selection for a generalized additive model (GAM). GAMs model the expected value of an outcome as the sum of smooth, independent functions for each predictor. GAMs are highly interpretable and can flexibly model non-linear relationships.

Data was filtered and grouped by each relevant site. For each sample, four columns were added: the first collection date for the site; days elapsed since that date; the log-transformed rolling 7-day average; and the day of the year.

GAM models were fit to a Gaussian distribution using the mgcv^25^ package. The predictors---days elapsed and day of the year---were both fit as a smoothed curve to explain the weekly mean log RSV concentration. "Days elapsed" was based in a thin plate regression spline, which determines knot locations based on the data. "Day of the year" was based in a cyclic cubic spline to account for seasonal trends. REML smoothing parameter estimation was used to reduce the likelihood of oversmoothing the data.

The significance of each smooth term was assessed using approximate p-values from the model summary.

```{r to_gam_format, include=FALSE}

packageVersion("mgcv")

to_gam <- 
  function(site_num) {
    
    data <- 
      vt_rsv |> 
      filter(site == site_num) |> 
      mutate(
        first_date = min(date),
        days_elapsed = as.numeric(date - first_date),
        day_of_year = yday(date),
        weekly_mean_log = log1p(mean_7day)
        )
    
    return(data)
    
  }

```


```{r gam_analysis, include=FALSE}

site_1965_for_gam <- to_gam('1965')
site_1966_for_gam <- to_gam('1966')
site_1975_for_gam <- to_gam('1975')

# Fit gam for site 1965
gam_1965 <- gam(
  weekly_mean_log ~ s(days_elapsed) + s(day_of_year, bs = 'cc'),
  data = site_1965_for_gam,
  method = 'REML'
)

# Fit gam for site 1966
gam_1966 <- gam(
  weekly_mean_log ~ s(days_elapsed) + s(day_of_year, bs = 'cc'),
  data = site_1966_for_gam,
  method = 'REML'
)

# Fit gam for site 1975
gam_1975 <- gam(
  weekly_mean_log ~ s(days_elapsed) + s(day_of_year, bs = 'cc'),
  data = site_1975_for_gam,
  method = 'REML'
)

```

``` {r gam_summary_table}

gam_table <- 
  data.frame(
    site = c(1965, 1966, 1975),
    
    s_days_elapsed_p = c(format.pval(summary(gam_1965)$s.pv[1], digits = 3, eps = 0.001),
                         format.pval(summary(gam_1966)$s.pv[1], digits = 3, eps = 0.001),
                         format.pval(summary(gam_1975)$s.pv[1], digits = 3, eps = 0.001)),

    s_day_of_year_p = c(format.pval(summary(gam_1965)$s.pv[2], digits = 3, eps = 0.001),
                        format.pval(summary(gam_1966)$s.pv[2], digits = 3, eps = 0.001),
                        format.pval(summary(gam_1975)$s.pv[2], digits = 3, eps = 0.001)),
    
    dev_expl = c(summary(gam_1965)$dev.expl,
                 summary(gam_1966)$dev.expl,
                 summary(gam_1975)$dev.expl),
    
    edf_days_elapsed = c(round(summary(gam_1965)$edf[1], digits = 2),
                         round(summary(gam_1966)$edf[1], digits = 2),
                         round(summary(gam_1975)$edf[1], digits = 2)),
    
    edf_day_of_year = c(round(summary(gam_1965)$edf[2], digits = 2),
                        round(summary(gam_1966)$edf[2], digits = 2),
                        round(summary(gam_1975)$edf[2], digits = 2))
  ) |> 
  mutate(
    across(-c(site, s_days_elapsed_p, s_day_of_year_p), ~ round(., digits = 2)),
    
    dev_expl = scales::label_percent(accuracy = 0.1)(dev_expl)
    )

```

``` {r gam_graphs, include=FALSE}

gam_graphed_1965 <- 
  wrap_plots(
  gratia::draw(gam_1965, select = 1, smooth_col = colors["1965"], residuals = TRUE) + 
    labs(
      title = "'Days Elapsed' Component",
      x = paste0("Days since ", format(site_1965_for_gam$date[1], "%B %d, %Y")),
      y = "Partial Effect (log scale)"
    )
  ) +
  wrap_plots(
    gratia::draw(gam_1965, select = 2, smooth_col = colors["1965"], residuals = TRUE) + 
      labs(
        title = "'Day of Year' Component",
        x = "Day of Year",
        y = NULL)
    )

gam_graphed_1966 <- 
  wrap_plots(
  gratia::draw(gam_1966, select = 1, smooth_col = colors["1966"], residuals = TRUE) + 
    labs(
      title = "'Days Elapsed' Component",
      x = paste0("Days since ", format(site_1966_for_gam$date[1], "%B %d, %Y")),
      y = "Partial Effect (log scale)"
    )
  ) +
  wrap_plots(
    gratia::draw(gam_1966, select = 2, smooth_col = colors["1966"], residuals = TRUE) + 
      labs(
        title = "'Day of Year' Component",
        x = "Day of Year",
        y = NULL)
    ) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "GAM Smoothed Components for Site 1966"
  )

gam_graphed_1975 <- 
  wrap_plots(
  gratia::draw(gam_1975, select = 1, smooth_col = colors["1975"], residuals = TRUE) + 
    labs(
      title = "'Days Elapsed' Component",
      x = paste0("Days since ", format(site_1975_for_gam$date[1], "%B %d, %Y")),
      y = "Partial Effect (log scale)"
    )
  ) +
  wrap_plots(
    gratia::draw(gam_1975, select = 2, smooth_col = colors["1975"], residuals = TRUE) + 
      labs(
        title = "'Day of Year' Component",
        x = "Day of Year",
        y = NULL)
    ) +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "GAM Smoothed Components for Site 1975"
  )

```

``` {r gam_analysis_nums}

# Get the onset and offset dates for the residual patterns in the GAM graph for site 1965
gam_off_season_onset_dates <- 
  c(site_1965_for_gam$date[1] + days(50),
    site_1965_for_gam$date[1] + days(400),
    site_1965_for_gam$date[1] + days(825))
gam_off_season_offset_dates <- 
  c(site_1965_for_gam$date[1] + days(225),
    site_1965_for_gam$date[1] + days(630),
    site_1965_for_gam$date[1] + days(1000))

# Get the peak dates for the residual patterns for the same graph
gam_off_season_peak_dates <- 
  c(site_1965_for_gam$date[1] + days(150),
    site_1965_for_gam$date[1] + days(500),
    site_1965_for_gam$date[1] + days(870))

# Set the year for averaging
year(gam_off_season_onset_dates) <- 2025
year(gam_off_season_offset_dates) <- 2025
year(gam_off_season_peak_dates) <- 2025

# Take the mean
gam_mean_off_season_onset <- mean(gam_off_season_onset_dates)
gam_mean_off_season_offset <- mean(gam_off_season_offset_dates)
gam_mean_off_season_peak <- mean(gam_off_season_peak_dates)

# Format the date
gam_mean_off_season_onset <- format(gam_mean_off_season_onset, "%B %d")
gam_mean_off_season_offset <- format(gam_mean_off_season_offset, "%B %d")
gam_mean_off_season_peak <- format(gam_mean_off_season_peak, "%B %d")

```

# Results

An initial overview of the data sought to visualize early patterns to note trend and seasonal observations, compare sites, and identify any initial limitations of further analysis.

``` {r visualize_exploratory, fig.topcaption = TRUE, fig.cap = "**Figure 4**. Exploratory data analysis of RSV wastewater concentrations,\n faceted by NWSS collection site. Colored lines represent the calculated seven-day rolling average.\n Note: per CDC guidance, RSV Concentrations should not be compared between sites due to different sample collection methods^21^; Vermont, 2023-26."}

exploratory

```

Exploratory visualizations revealed clear seasonal patterns across sites consistent with known RSV epidemiology. Concentrations generally begin to increase in the fall and peak typically in January or February before declining again in April to May. This pattern was consistent across all sites. Only sites 1965, 1966, and 1975 contained three full seasons worth of data, providing enough data for further statistical tests (seasonal decomposition and GAM).

## Season Info {.tabset}

The following tables summarize seasonal characteristics across Vermont's NWSS monitoring sites, including season lengths, season peaks, peak years per site, and season onset and offset dates. As of May 19, 2026, the 2025-26 season was still ongoing at sites `r vt_rsv_seasonal |> distinct(site, season, onset, offset) |> filter(season != "Off-Season", is.na(offset)) |> pull(site) |> paste(collapse = ", ")` in Vermont.

### Season lengths (Table 1)

``` {r season_lengths}

szn_lengths |> 
  
  mutate(

    across(-site, ~ as.character(.)),
    across(-site, ~ replace_na(., "—")),
    site = case_when(
      
      site == 'avg_szn_length_per_year' ~ "Avg. / season (all sites)",
      site == 'avg_szn_length_per_year_excl_low_data' ~ "Avg. / season (excl. low-data sites)", 
      TRUE ~ site 
      
    )
    
  ) |> 
  
  kable(caption = "<b>Table 1.</b> Season lengths by site, including the averaged length.",
        col.names = c("Site", "2022-23", "2023-24", "2024-25", "2025-26", "Avg. / site (all years)", "Avg. / site (excl. 2022-23)"),
        format = "html",
        escape = FALSE) |> 
  
  kable_styling(position = "center") |> 
  
  column_spec(c(6, 7), include_thead = TRUE, color = 'steelblue') |> 
  
  row_spec(c(7, 8), color = 'steelblue')

```

Season lengths for 2022-23 are inaccurate due to large gaps in data for that season; data collection did not begin until late into the season's latter half. Sites 1967, 1976, 2350 have extremely low coverage---these sites were not used for further analysis due to insufficient data to meet the onset and offset detection threshold. Site 1975 had the longest seasons recorded, and season lengths were generally similar between sites 1965, 1966, and 1975. Across seasons, lengths were also relatively similar on average, excluding the ongoing 2025-26 and undercovered 2022-23 seasons. 

### Season peaks (Table 2)

``` {r peak_day}

peak_days |> 
  
  mutate(
    
    across(-site, ~ as.character(.)),
    across(-site, ~ replace_na(., "—")),
    site = if_else(
      site == "avg_peak_day_per_season",
      "Avg. / season",
      site
    )
    
  ) |> 
  
  kable(caption = "<b>Table 2.</b> Season peak days by site, including the average peak by season and site.",
        col.names = c("Site", "2022-23", "2023-24", "2024-25", "2025-26", "Avg. / site")) |> 
  
  kable_styling(position = "center") |> 
  
  column_spec(6, include_thead = TRUE, color = 'steelblue') |> 
  
  row_spec(7, color = 'steelblue')


```

Seasonal peaks most commonly fall in early to mid-February. In 2025-26, the peak fell a month later in mid-March.

### Peak years (Table 3)

``` {r peak_year}

peak_year |> 
  
  mutate(
    
    across(-site, ~ as.character(.)),
    across(-site, ~ replace_na(., "—")),
    year = str_replace_all(year, "_", "-")
    
  ) |> 
  
  kable(caption = "<b>Table 3.</b> Peak year for each site.",
        col.names = c("Site", "Year")) |> 
  
  kable_styling(position = "center")

```

The most common year with the highest peak for each site was 2023-24, followed by 2025-26. Site 1967's result is trivial since the site is limited to one year of data.

### Season onsets and offsets (Table 4)

``` {r onset_offset}

onset_offset |> 
  
  mutate(
    average_onset = if_else(
      season == '2022_2023',
      "Low data",
      as.character(average_onset)
    ),
    
    average_offset = if_else(
      season == '2025_2026',
      "Ongoing",
      as.character(average_offset)
    ),
    across(-season, ~ str_replace_all(., "_", "-"))
  ) |> 
  
  kable(caption = "<b>Table 4.</b> Average season onset and offset each year.",
        col.names = c("Season", "Average onset", "Average offset")) |> 
  
  kable_styling(position = "center")

```

The 2022-23 season did not have enough data to attribute an onset date. The 2023-24 and 2024-25 seasons both began in mid to late December and ended in mid to late April. The 2022-23 season also offset in mid to late April, which may suggest greater data coverage would indicate season onset was similar to the following two seasons. The 2025-26 season began 2-3 weeks later in mid-January and was still ongoing as of May 19, 2026.

## STL Decomposition and GAM

``` {r visualize_seasonal_offset, fig.topcaption = TRUE, fig.cap = "**Figure 5**. RSV wastewater concentrations in Vermont by day since the start of the on-season; Vermont, 2023-26"}

season_trend

```

Sites 1965, 1966, and 1975 all showed high spikes in RSV concentration during mid-late season 2023-24. RSV concentrations were highest overall in the 2023-24 season in Chittenden county, while the 2025-26 season concentrations were the highest in Washington county (Figure 5).

``` {r visualize_seasonal_peaks, fig.topcaption = TRUE, fig.cap = "**Figure 6**. RSV wastewater concentrations in Vermont by day since June 1st of the previous off-season; Vermont, 2023-26", out.width = "600px"}

season_offset

```

Generally, seasons occurring earlier in the data collection periods exhibit earlier season onset and peak earlier. The 2023-24 season rises and peaks early across all sites before the 2024-25 and 2025-26 seasons rise. Peaks in the latter seasons appear to be between 30-50 days later than peaks in the 2023-24 season (Figure 6). 

``` {r show_seasonal_decomposition, fig.topcaption = TRUE, fig.cap = "**Figure 7**. STL decomposition of log weekly RSV wastewater concentration over the observation period at NWSS site 1965; Chittenden County, Vermont, 2023-26"}

decomposed_1965
  
```

Seasonal decomposition showed seasonal peaks occur on average on `r peak_as_month_date(decomp_peaks_1965)`, `r peak_as_month_date(decomp_peaks_1966)`, and `r peak_as_month_date(decomp_peaks_1975)` for sites 1965, 1966, and 1975 respectively. Site 1965 showed a dip in the overall trend of the data during summer 2024. From there the trend continues to increase steadily throughout 2025 and early 2026, where it is currently highest (Figure 7). Site 1966 peaked overall during the 2023-24 respiratory virus season and steadily decreased until the start of the 2025-26 season. The trend for site 1975 fluctuates slightly over time, but shows an increasing trend in the 2025-26 season (S1). All sites showed a clear seasonal cycle alongside a non-linear long-term trend.

``` {r}

gam_table |> 
  
  kable(caption = "<b>Table 5.</b> GAM model summary statistics for sites 1965, 1966, and 1975. All smooth terms are significant at p < 0.001.",
        col.names = c("Site", "Trend p-value", "Seasonality p-value", "Deviance explained", "EDF for trend", "EDF for seasonality"))

```

GAM identified statistically significant long-term trends and seasonal smooth terms at sites 1965, 1966, and 1975 (all p < 2e-16, or p $\approx$ `r mean(c(summary(gam_1965)$s.pv, summary(gam_1966)$s.pv, summary(gam_1975)$s.pv))`), with average degrees of freedom of `r round(mean(c(summary(gam_1965)$edf[1], summary(gam_1966)$edf[1], summary(gam_1975)$edf[1])), digits = 2)` and `r round(mean(c(summary(gam_1965)$edf[2], summary(gam_1966)$edf[2], summary(gam_1975)$edf[2])), digits = 2)` for trend and seasonality, respectively. Site 1975 had the highest deviance explained, followed by 1966, and 1965 (Table 5).

``` {r gam_graphed, fig.topcaption = TRUE, fig.cap = "**Figure 8**. Generalized additive model (GAM) smoothed components for RSV wastewater concentration over the observation period at NWSS site 1965; Chittenden County, Vermont, 2023-26"}

gam_graphed_1965

```

The partial effect of the 'days elapsed' component for site 1965's GAM shows a trend with relative peaks around `r format(site_1965_for_gam$date[1] + days(230), "%B %d, %Y")` and `r format(site_1965_for_gam$date[1] + days(760), "%B %d, %Y")`. In the 2025-26 season, the data increases towards an absolute maximum as of May 19, 2026. The seasonal component shows a peak in RSV concentrations within the first fifty days of the year, with a sharp decrease that reaches a minimum around mid-July. Concentrations begin to rise again gradually, before sharply increasing beginning in late October.

During off-season periods, residual plotting revealed patterns of large negative residuals indicating model overprediction of near-zero values. These periods were also accompanied by wider confidence intervals, corroborating the model's limitations in predicting near-zero concentrations. During on-season periods, concentration spikes occasionally exceeded the model's prediction. Similar patterns in trend were observed for sites 1966 and 1975 (Figure S2).

# Limitations

The CDC advised against comparison of RSV concentrations across sites due to differences in collection methods, collection frequencies, and laboratory testing methods^13^. Exploratory analysis found that sites 1967, 1976, and 2350 had large gaps in data that affected seasonal classification using the method defined in Boehm et al.^2,23,24^ These sites were not analyzed further using STL decomposition and GAM due to low coverage. The 2022-23 season was also excluded due to low coverage. The first date on record for any site was `r format(first_collection_date, "%B %d, %Y")`---3 months into a typical season, which undermined the validity of onset date for the 2022-23 season.

In both component graphs of the GAM, large negative residuals in the off-season (around `r gam_mean_off_season_onset` to `r gam_mean_off_season_offset`) reflect systematic overprediction of near-zero values by the model---this is a known limitation of Gaussian family distributions in epidemic modeling^26^. Towards the beginning and end of each off-season the magnitude of the model's overprediction is greatest since it expects a continuous distribution. Future analyses may benefit from zero-inflated or hurdle model frameworks^26^, which are frequently used in epidemic analysis pipelines due to their ability to account for excess zeros in the data.

Clinical data for RSV hospitalizations were not available for Vermont, precluding validation of wastewater trends against clinical outcomes. Wastewater viral concentrations and clinical outcomes have been correlated in previous studies^9,11^; hospitalization data could cross-validate these findings and strengthen interpretation of wastewater trends as indicators of community disease burden.

# Discussion

For the 2025-26 season, the CDC indicated that the national RSV burden was not likely to be more severe compared to previous seasons^12^. However, Vermont only represents about 0.2% of the US population; an increase in the state's RSV burden would contribute minimally to national aggregated data. This could obscure critical state-level variation for low-population states in national surveillance reports. 

Excluding low-coverage sites 1967, 1976, and 2350, only site 1975 showed (Figure 5) an absolute peak for the 2025-26 season (Table 3). Interestingly, while seasonal decomposition showed only site 1965 had an increasing trend in the 2025-26 season (Figure 7, S1), GAM showed sites 1965, 1966, and 1975 all climbed towards absolute maxima in the 'days elapsed' component (Figure 8, S2), indicating higher RSV wastewater concentrations in the 2025-26 season. The smoothing splines approach in GAM better detected trends that LOESS local smoothing underweighted.

While RSV wastewater concentrations in the 2025-26 season were elevated relative to prior years, this cannot be interpreted as increased disease burden without considering viral shedding dynamics. Wastewater viral concentrations reflect the volume shed by individuals in a community ^9,10,27^, which has been found to vary by age, symptom severity, and infection status ^28,29^. Wastewater concentrations may have been higher this season due to variation in viral shedding based on who was infected and the conditions under which transmission occurred.

RSV season begins in fall and peaks in December or January ^1,2^, lasting 3-5 months on average in temperate regions ^30,31^ such as Vermont. Vermont's WBE data indicated seasons lasted `r round(szn_lengths[8,7]$avg_szn_length_per_site_excl_22_23 / 30, digits = 2)` months on average from 2023-26 (Table 1), began in mid-late December, ended in mid-late April (Table 4), and peaked in January or February (Table 2). The 2025-26 season strayed from these trends; it began later, peaked in late February to early April, and was still ongoing as of May 19, 2026 (Figure 7-8, S1-S2). Discrepancies in season duration have been found to be important in inferring the burden of disease and allocating resources in a community^2^.

Recent winters have been unpredictable in onset and severity of conditions^32^. Notably, the 2025-26 winter had a delayed onset and was especially cold, which may influence the spread of RSV. Generally, inclement climates increase exposure by forcing people together inside ^30^. Dry, cold winters are known to encourage the stability and transmission of airborne viruses, including RSV, as water evaporates from respiratory particles, decreasing volume and increasing aerosolization^33,34,35,36^. RSV survives longer on fomites in these conditions, which are a primary source of infection^34,37^. In humans, cold air inhalation also limits metabolic functions that assist in clearing mucus and the activity of innate immune cells^33^.

RSV transmission was reduced in 2020 following nationwide nonpharmaceutical COVID-19 interventions ^38,39^. These changes also disrupted subsequent seasons. In 2021-22, RSV season began earlier and lasted longer. In 2022-23, the season also began earlier ^39,40^. These recent temporal shifts may predict a return to pre-pandemic seasonality as population immunity and viral circulation adapt and reach a new equilibrium. A late winter could disrupt the change in this equilibrium, potentially contributing to Vermont's higher RSV wastewater concentrations in 2025-26.

Viral shedding decreases with age^29^, however infants protected through vaccination or protective medicines such as nirsevimab have lower risks of infection and severity of symptoms; immunized infants shed less virus. Vaccination data in Vermont indicated infants made up significantly less of the vulnerable population and were more likely to be vaccinated for RSV than older adults^2^. 

Nirsevimab is a monoclonal antibody that protects infants for around 5 months^41,42^. This medicine is most frequently administered to infants younger than 8 months, before their first RSV season — usually in October. Late peaks in the 2025-26 season often occurred in late February or later (Table 2, Figure 6). Considering infected infants shed more RSV than older adults, these late peaks would coincide with nirsevimab immunity expiring 5 months after initial dosing in October 2025.

All PCR data collected from Vermont's NWSS sites was aggregated for RSV-A and RSV-B. RSV-A is known to have higher rates of shedding in infected individuals^26^. Further analysis could provide insight to geographic and temporal changes in the prevalence of each strain to determine if higher prevalence of RSV-A influenced increased wastewater concentrations.

## Implications for Public Health Practice

Annual state guidelines could provide recommendations for the timing of seasonal nirsevimab doses for infants based on the start of the respiratory virus season. Providing the protective medicine in November or early December could provide better coverage during the RSV season (Table 2 and 4). This approach may be essential to help manage limited resources while maintaining infant immunity for as long as possible during relevant times. Dosing is only recommended once a season^43^, however, careful consideration of repeat dosing protocols for extended seasons may be warranted.

As of May 2026, no national eligibility criteria exist for access to RSV testing^9^. This gap is exacerbated by existing barriers to healthcare for at-risk populations^9^. Centralized eligibility criteria based on age, symptoms, and risk-group may lower testing barriers for patients. Vermont may benefit from centralized criteria in-state to reduce barriers to timely diagnosis for providers and at-risk patients.

Furthermore, access to hospitalization data would be critical to understand clinical implications of WBE. Prior studies have found correlation between WBE concentration peaks and hospitalization rates^9^, and that delayed wastewater peaks relative to hospitalization rates may reflect infected individuals not seeking care^10^. This is especially important to consider in rural communities, where Vermont residents have been found to have travel times up to 3.6 times longer to a healthcare facility^27^. Understanding the correlation between WBE and clinical outcomes may allow public health officials to better infer the burden of disease and allocate limited resources such as vaccines and tests^31^ while also identifying gaps in healthcare access for at-risk populations.

Generalizability is a recognized strength of WBE^2^, however Vermont does not have enough participating NWSS sites to adequately monitor RSV burden across all of Vermont's rural counties. Increasing the number of participating NWSS sites in Vermont would allow better generalization to underrepresented rural communities that are far from current state NWSS sites. Implementation of strain-specific surveillance at each monitoring site would enable correlation analyses between RSV strain prevalence, wastewater concentrations, and disease burden.

# Conclusion

This analysis identified elevated RSV wastewater concentrations and a delayed 2025-26 season across Vermont NWSS monitoring sites. The 2025-26 season was still ongoing as of May 19, 2026, and continued monitoring is warranted to fully characterize the season's trajectory. Expanding Vermont's wastewater surveillance infrastructure and integrating clinical outcome data into public databases would substantially improve the state's capacity to monitor and respond to RSV in future seasons as RSV seasonality returns to pre-pandemic patterns. Annual seasonality data should inform public health guidelines for immunization timelines and testing protocols.

# Sources

1. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Respiratory Syncytial Virus. https://www.cdc.gov/rsv/about/index.html. Updated December 30, 2025. Accessed May 14, 2026.

2. Chan EMG, Boehm AB. Respiratory Virus Season Surveillance in the United States Using
Wastewater Metrics, 2023–2024. ACS ES&T Water. 2025;5(2):985-992.
DOI: https://doi.org/10.1021/acsestwater.4c01013.

3. Davido B, Loubet P. The silent surge: The under-recognised burden of respiratory syncytial virus, human metapneumovirus, and Parainfluenza viruses in adults. International Journal of Infectious Diseases. 2025;159:108006. DOI: http://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijid.2025.108006.

4. Melgar M, Britton A, Roper LE, et al. Use of Respiratory Syncytial Virus Vaccines in Older Adults: Recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices — United States, 2023. MMWR Morb Mortal Wkly Rep 2023;72:793–801. DOI: http://dx.doi.org/10.15585/mmwr.mm7229a4.

5. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Flu Burden. https://www.cdc.gov/flu-burden/php/about/index.html. Updated February 25, 2026. Accessed May 13, 2026.

6. Recto CG, Fourati S, Khellaf M, et al. Respiratory Syncytial Virus vs Influenza Virus Infection: Mortality and Morbidity Comparison Over 7 Epidemic Seasons in an Elderly Population. J Infect Dis. 2024;230(5):1130-1138. DOI: http://dx.doi.org/10.1093/infdis/jiae171

7. https://www.cdc.gov/rsv/php/surveillance/rsv-net.html

8. Adams C, Bias M, Welsh RM, et al. The National Wastewater Surveillance System (NWSS): From inception to widespread coverage, 2020-2022, United States. Sci Total Environ. 2024;924:171566. DOI: http://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2024.171566.

9. Zulli A, Varkila MRJ, Parsonnet J, Wolfe MK, Boehm AB. Observations of Respiratory
Syncytial Virus (RSV) Nucleic Acids in Wastewater Solids Across the United States in the
2022–2023 Season: Relationships with RSV Infection Positivity and Hospitalization Rates.
ACS ES&T Water. 2024;4(6):2385-2394. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1021/acsestwater.3c00725.

10. Viviani L, Vecchio R, Villa S, et al. Wastewater epidemiology of respiratory viruses: implementation of a regional surveillance system. Eur J Public Health. 2025;35(Suppl 4):ckaf161.742. Published 2025 Oct 27. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/eurpub/ckaf161.742.

11. Ensor K, Palacio J, Keller S, et al. Understanding transmission and infections of respiratory syncytial virus through wastewater-based epidemiology. Environmental Science: Water Research & Technology. 2026;12;1600-1606. Published 2026 Mar 17. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1039/D5EW01170A.

12. Verani M, Pagani A, Federigi I, et al. Wastewater-Based Epidemiology for Viral Surveillance from an Endemic Perspective: Evidence and Challenges. Viruses. 2024;16(3):482. Published 2024 Mar 20. DOI: https://doi.org/10.3390/v16030482.

13. Champredon D, Vanrolleghem PA. Editorial: Wastewater-based epidemiological surveillance of respiratory pathogens. Front Public Health. 2023;11:1328452. Published 2023 Nov 16. DOI:
https://doi.org/10.3389/fpubh.2023.1328452.

14. Midgley CM, Haynes AK, Baumgardner JL, et al. Determining the Seasonality of Respiratory Syncytial Virus in the United States: The Impact of Increased Molecular Testing. J Infect Dis. 2017;216(3):345-355. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/infdis/jix275.

15. Belongia EA, King JP, Kieke BA, et al. Clinical Features, Severity, and Incidence of RSV Illness During 12 Consecutive Seasons in a Community Cohort of Adults ≥60 Years Old. Open Forum Infect Dis. 2018;5(12):ofy316. Published 2018 Nov 27. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/ofid/ofy316.

16. https://dcf.vermont.gov/cdd-blog/rsv-testing-care-information

17. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. CDC's Wastewater Monitoring Program. https://www.cdc.gov/wastewater/about/index.html?cove-tab=3. Updated May 13, 2026. Accessed May 14, 2026.

18. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. The National Respiratory and Enteric Virus Surveillance System (NREVSS). https://www.cdc.gov/nrevss/php/dashboard/index.html. Updated June 18, 2025. Accessed May 14, 2026.

19. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Respiratory Illnesses. https://www.cdc.gov/respiratory-viruses/data/. Updated May 20, 2026. Accessed May 21, 2026.

20. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. CFA: Qualitative. https://www.cdc.gov/cfa-qualitative-assessments/php/data-research/season-outlook25-26.html. Updated May 20, 2026. Accessed May 21, 2026.

21. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. CDC Wastewater Data for RSV. https://data.cdc.gov/Public-Health-Surveillance/CDC-Wastewater-Data-for-RSV/45cq-cw4i/about_data. Updated May 18, 2026. Accessed May 19, 2026.

22. https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/volumes/72/wr/mm7214a1.htm

23. Zulli A, Varkila MRJ, Parsonnet J, Wolfe MK, Boehm AB. Observations of Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) Nucleic Acids in Wastewater Solids Across the United States in the 2022-2023 Season: Relationships with RSV Infection Positivity and Hospitalization Rates. ACS ES T Water. 2024;4(4):1657-1667. Published 2024 Feb 29. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1021/acsestwater.3c00725.

24. Boehm AB, Wolfe MK, White BJ, Hughes B, Duong D, Bidwell A. More than a Tripledemic: Influenza A Virus, Respiratory Syncytial Virus, SARS-CoV-2, and Human Metapneumovirus in Wastewater during Winter 2022-2023. Environ Sci Technol Lett. 2023;10(8):622-627. Published 2023 Jul 20. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.estlett.3c00385.

25. Wood SN. Fast stable restricted maximum likelihood and marginal likelihood estimation of semiparametric generalized linear models. J R Stat Soc Series B Stat Methodol. 2011;73(1):3-36. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9868.2010.00749.x.

26. A. Lu J, Meyer S. A zero-inflated endemic-epidemic model with an application to measles time series in Germany. Biom J. 2023;65(8):e2100408. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/bimj.202100408.

27. Lowry SA, Wolfe MK, Boehm AB. Respiratory virus concentrations in human excretions that contribute to wastewater: a systematic review and meta-analysis. J Water Health. 2023;21(6):831-848. DOI: https://doi.org/10.2166/wh.2023.057.

28. Otomaru H, Sornillo JBT, Kamigaki T, et al. Risk of Transmission and Viral Shedding From the Time of Infection for Respiratory Syncytial Virus in Households. Am J Epidemiol. 2021;190(12):2536-2543. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/aje/kwab181.

29. Munywoki PK, Koech DC, Agoti CN, et al. Influence of age, severity of infection, and co-infection on the duration of respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) shedding. Epidemiol Infect. 2015;143(4):804-812. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1017/S0950268814001393.

30. Janet S, Broad J, Snape MD. Respiratory syncytial virus seasonality and its implications on prevention strategies. Hum Vaccin Immunother. 2018;14(1):234-244. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1080/21645515.2017.1403707.

31. Staadegaard L, Caini S, Wangchuk S, et al. Defining the seasonality of respiratory syncytial virus around the world: National and subnational surveillance data from 12 countries. Influenza Other Respir Viruses. 2021;15(6):732-741. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1111/irv.12885.

32. https://climatechange.vermont.gov/vermont-today

33. Lowen AC, Steel J. Roles of humidity and temperature in shaping influenza seasonality. J Virol. 2014;88(14):7692-7695. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1128/JVI.03544-13.

34. Paynter S. Humidity and respiratory virus transmission in tropical and temperate settings. Epidemiol Infect. 2015;143(6):1110-1118. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1017/S0950268814002702.

35. Nenna R, Evangelisti M, Frassanito A, et al. Respiratory syncytial virus bronchiolitis, weather conditions and air pollution in an Italian urban area: An observational study. Environ Res. 2017;158:188-193. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envres.2017.06.014.

36. Cui, B., Zhang, D., Pan, H. et al. Viral aetiology of acute respiratory infections among children and associated meteorological factors in southern China. BMC Infect Dis 15, 124 (2015). DOI: https://doi.org/10.1186/s12879-015-0863-6.

37. Hall CB, Douglas RG, Geiman JM. Possible transmission by fomites of respiratory syncytial virus. Journal of Infectious Diseases 1980; 141: 98–102. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/infdis/141.1.98.

38. https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/volumes/70/wr/mm7029a1.htm

39. Thindwa D, Li K, Cooper-Wootton D, Zheng Z, Pitzer VE, Weinberger DM. Global patterns of rebound to normal RSV dynamics following COVID-19 suppression. BMC Infect Dis. 2024;24(1):635. Published 2024 Jun 25. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1186/s12879-024-09509-4.

40. https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/volumes/72/wr/mm7214a1.htm

41. https://www.healthvermont.gov/stats/data-reporting-topic/respiratory-virus-vaccination-data

42. https://www.mayoclinic.org/drugs-supplements/nirsevimab-alip-intramuscular-route/description/drg-20552487.

43. https://www.cdc.gov/rsv/hcp/vaccine-clinical-guidance/infants-young-children.html

``` {r supplemental_table_1}

# Site populations
vt_rsv |> 
  select(site, population_served) |> 
  distinct(site, .keep_all = TRUE) |> 
  kable(caption = "<b>Table S1.</b> The population served by each participating NWSS site in Vermont.") |>
  kable_styling(position = "center")

```

``` {r supplemental_figs}

# Decomposition graphs for sites 1966 and 1975
decomposed_1966 +
  decomposed_1975 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "STL Decomposition of RSV Wastewater Concentration",
    caption = "Source: CDC National Wastewater Surveillance System"
  )

summary(gam_1966)
summary(gam_1975)

gam_graphed_1966
gam_graphed_1975

```

---
*Correspondence: Jacob Pancoast, University of Vermont | jacob.pancoast@uvm.edu*
*Data: CDC National Wastewater Surveillance System (data.cdc.gov)*
*The author declares no conflicts of interest.*
